variables.name: Leading-edge AI chip access (the near-term chokepoint)

actor_readings.reading: Access exists but is licensed, monitored (#70) and capped (#71); binding today, cite #3 #5 #7 #139.
Cut off from top-tier NVIDIA; forced onto Ascend and smuggling — driving indigenization pressure, cite #60 #66.
Constrained by capital not licenses — cannot afford scale even when allowed, cite #100 #11.

variables.current_reading: Gulf access is US-licensed and conditional: G42/HUMAIN NVIDIA deals were approved only under geopolitical pressure and remain revocable (#5, #7). Grey-market transshipment through UAE/Singapore/Malaysia is documented and material (#139). No indigenous supply. Confidence high that access = US-gated today.
variables.trajectory: Widening but on-sufferance. Chinese substitute (Ascend 910C ~A100-tier) is closing, raising the credible outside option (#60, #12). Architectural downgrades (H20 NVLink caps, #71) and on-chip attestation (#70) mean licensed chips carry immutable/monitored ceilings. Clock: like oil scarcity (A3) it is POLICY-determined, so reversible in weeks by Washington — unlike geological scarcity — making the lever fragile but fast-moving.
variables.base_rate: Resource-chokepoint leverage (A3 OPEC, A21 Shinkolobwe uranium) is real but time-boxed to ~7–20 years until diversification/substitution; analogy breaks because compute is policy-scarce not geologically scarce, so the window can close by decision, not depletion.

variables.name: Upstream silicon stack (fab / EUV / EDA / packaging / CUDA)

actor_readings.reading: THIS is China's minimum stave — blocked at EUV/EDA regardless of Ascend progress, cite #66 #127 #134.
Zero indigenous fab; any 'pole' holds chips-on-sufferance from Taiwan (#17), unresolvable by capital.
Controls the stack via allies (NL/Japan/Korea/Taiwan) — the durable source of all downstream leverage.

variables.current_reading: Fully US-allied-controlled and the deepest lock. ASML EUV monopoly (#127), Synopsys/Cadence EDA (#134), TSMC/SK-Hynix CoWoS+HBM packaging (#117), and NVIDIA CUDA software moat (#135) each sit upstream of any 'fab independence.' TSMC makes ~90% of leading-edge (A7). No non-US-allied path exists. Confidence very high.
variables.trajectory: Slowly diversifying geographically (TSMC Arizona, JASM Japan, Germany 2026–2030, #49) but CHIPS Act conditionality means diversification = dependency-transfer, not independence (#124). Clock: slowest-moving variable — decade-scale, catch-up historically hardest layer (A5 Japan shows even an ally with real fabs was subordinated; A13 shows software/messaging layers fragment faster than hardware).
variables.base_rate: Semiconductor leadership (A5) took Japan 20+ years to build and was reversed by trade policy in a decade; fab/tooling is the least catch-up-able input in tech-race history. Analogy breaks only if algorithmic efficiency (#123) collapses the compute threshold.

variables.name: Export-control regime durability (the US policy chokepoint)

actor_readings.reading: Owns the lever but faces internal erosion (NVIDIA revenue #136, #129 reverse-entanglement) — leverage is self-undermining.
Primary route to relax the chip constraint via energy/basing/finance bargaining (#13 #52) — partial success already (#5).

variables.current_reading: Contested and softening at the margin. BIS diffusion rule + Tier caps are the live chokepoint (#5), but G42/HUMAIN approvals show relaxation under pressure (#5, #7). NVIDIA lobbying + transactional US politics erode it from within (#136); WTO challenge adds a third contest channel (#151). Confidence medium — direction genuinely uncertain.
variables.trajectory: Two opposing vectors: hardening options unused (API/service-layer controls #116, packaging #117, attestation mandates #70) vs. erosion (lobbying #136, deal-making, secondary-sanctions only if Taiwan crisis, #56). Clock: fast — a single administration can flip it. A25 petrodollar shows the US will trade access for structural integration.
variables.base_rate: Ally strategic-sector controls (A5 Japan STA) held when they served US commercial interest and were relaxed when politically bought (A25); base rate is that the hegemon keeps the lever but sells exceptions transactionally.

variables.name: Training-capability breakout threshold

actor_readings.reading: Inference-only now; incentive structure may produce Potemkin capability not a training trajectory (#77) — but breakout is a live tail (#43).
Already training-capable via Mistral + EuroHPC (#82 #85) — the bipolar frame is descriptively wrong on this axis.
Fiscally capped at government-use-case depth, not commercial/frontier scale (#100).

variables.current_reading: Gulf sits at inference-hosting + early mid-scale; not frontier-training-capable (#4, #77). This is THE crux separating real pole from sophisticated customer — 'nuclear plant vs. the bomb' (#4). EU (Mistral + EuroHPC) already crosses into mid/frontier-adjacent (#82, #85). Confidence high for Gulf, medium for EU.
variables.trajectory: Threshold is FALLING fast: MoE, distillation, synthetic data cut capex (#123, #142); breakout may be discontinuous — pre-positioned inference assets convert 'overnight' on a chip/fab/smuggling trigger (#43). But permanent silicon caps (#71) and talent (#8) can keep it a moat. Clock: contested — could arrive at an order-of-magnitude lower capex than 2023 estimates.
variables.base_rate: Meiji Japan (A19) and Sweden (A28) show indigenous capability is buildable but requires domesticating knowledge, not just hosting advisors; NPT (A27) shows the 'civilian-access' tier is easy to enter and hard to graduate from. Analogy breaks if algorithmic deflation makes training cheap.

variables.name: Great-power bidding / credibility of a second pole (China as outside option)

actor_readings.reading: US's leverage-through-access DEPENDS on China staying non-credible; DeepSeek-class parity at the application layer (#64) is the crux that could void US AI-access leverage.
Active suitor via DSR/open-weights (#58 #59 #61) but hobbled by upstream silicon (V2) and export playbook deficit (#33).
Extracts rent from the rivalry (#35 #58); needs a credible Chinese backup to keep the threat live (#12).

variables.current_reading: Currently a genuine auction: DSR is physically installed in 100+ countries (#59), Ascend is an A100-tier fallback (#60), China's deliberate open-weight strategy (DeepSeek/Qwen) diffuses its stack under export controls (#61, #64). BUT China is a weak third-country AI exporter (#33) and its conditionality is not obviously lower (gallium controls #66, e-CNY #125). Confidence medium that the auction is real NOW.
variables.trajectory: Sustained near-term by active Chinese suitorship (#58) — the one NAM condition that historically worked (A1/A10/A14 Tito/Nasser/Vargas extracted from BOTH sides). Erodes if US pulls decisively ahead (A2/A10/A22 endpoint = forced alignment). Clock: tied to relative US-China capability gap; the swing window is inherently ~2024–2030 (#10).
variables.base_rate: NAM leverage (A1) survived exactly as long as competitive superpower bidding did and collapsed with the USSR (1991); Thailand (A22) and Egypt (A10) bent fully once one pole dominated. Base rate: auction leverage is real but ends abruptly when bidding becomes one-sided.

variables.name: Financial + hard-security umbrella binding (US leverage over the Gulf)

actor_readings.reading: MINIMUM STAVE near-term — finance/security is a harder constraint than compute (#6 #15 #46 #55); self-undermining hedging loop (#55) may foreclose stable semi-alignment.
NATO Art.5 / post-Ukraine US dependence binds EU digital autonomy at least as tightly (#86).

variables.current_reading: Hard binding today and the likeliest true Gulf minimum stave. Dollar/SWIFT embeds SWF solvency (#15, #32, #141); CENTCOM basing + Patriot/THAAD + Iran deterrence create existential security dependence (#46); arms-sale conditionality is a repeatable lever (#47, F-35/G42 episode #6); Abraham Accords deepen the compact (#51). The 2024 G42 Huawei divestiture is the concrete data point that binary pressure forced a choice (#6). Confidence high.
variables.trajectory: Structurally sticky but not deterministic: Turkey/Egypt (#54) show issue-specific defection at manageable cost; e-CNY/mBridge offers partial settlement escape (#125); reverse-entanglement (US tech revenue + SWF stakes, #129) constrains the constrainer. Clock: slow-moving, changes over decades (A25 petrodollar = 50 years).
variables.base_rate: Finlandisation (A18) and petrodollar (A25) show formally sovereign states accommodate the security/finance hegemon for decades; Sweden (A28) shows 210 years of neutrality can end in 18 months under one large shock. Base rate strongly favors binding; analogy breaks because the Gulf is a capital EXPORTER (unlike Djibouti A23), inverting the usual dependency.

variables.name: Complementary inputs — talent, data flywheel, operational workforce

actor_readings.reading: Long-run capability CEILING (#8 #24 #34 #74); niche Arabic moat is real but sub-frontier (#41).
Has talent but hemorrhages it to US labs (#87) — worse than Gulf because it has capability to lose.
Data/language moats (Hindi/Bahasa/Portuguese/Hausa #95; genomic #149) are their structural advantage; diaspora entangles India with US (#96).

variables.current_reading: Gulf is shallow and import-dependent: no deep research community (#8), ~80–90% expatriate ops workforce on revocable visas (#74), ~45M people = no proprietary data flywheel (#24, #34). Partial fixes exist: Arabic/Islamic niche moat (#41), compute-for-data swaps (#37), federated learning (#148). Population-scale states have the inverse profile — data-rich, capital-poor (#138, #95). Confidence high.
variables.trajectory: Structural ceiling capital cannot fully buy (#8, #24); but synthetic data (#142) and bilateral data swaps (#37, #114) could convert it from ceiling to procurement problem. Population-scale states' organic flywheel COMPOUNDS over 10–20 yrs while Gulf deficit persists (#138). Clock: slowest, human-capital compounding is the least catch-up-able factor (A19 Meiji required mass education).
variables.base_rate: Every historical pole (US/China) rested on a large domestic market driving data/talent flywheels; A19 Meiji shows knowledge must be domesticated, not hosted. Analogy breaks if synthetic data (#142) makes organic data optional — the single biggest uncertainty here.

variables.name: Neutrality-premium / dual-stack hub viability (rent from bifurcation)

actor_readings.reading: Core value proposition and most defensible asset (#35 #42 #44); but requires verifiable neutrality governance it has not yet built (A15/A16), else it is a leased venue not a trusted one.

variables.current_reading: Operative and partially self-reinforcing: Gulf hosts US hyperscalers + (formerly) Chinese stacks; the worse the bifurcation the more valuable the neutral venue (#35, #42). Hyperscaler energy/land hunger inverts dependency into supplier power (#39). BUT hosting Western hyperscalers = latent Five Eyes exposure (#137, #22), and G42 showed the dual-stack broke under pressure (#6). Confidence medium.
variables.trajectory: Self-reinforcing IF neutrality is proven not just declared (#42, Swiss-banking logic) — but disciplined by asset hyperconcentration/kinetic-cyber vulnerability (#78) and the security-logic that forbids stable semi-alignment (#55). Clock: leverage peaks now; switching-cost moats (#45) may outlast the training-race window even if training leverage collapses.
variables.base_rate: Neutral-hub precedents are the MOST optimistic: Venice ~800 yrs (A26), Hansa ~300 yrs (A17), Singapore/Austria/BIS durable (A15/A16/A24) — but all required institutional credibility that A11 (Hong Kong) shows evaporates when the stronger neighbor no longer needs the interface. Analogy breaks: compute hubs need a non-neutral GPU supplier (A6 — 'no neutral accelerator supplier').

variables.name: Capability-diffusion shape (the leverage clock)

actor_readings.reading: Winner-take-all is the US's best case (leverage concentrates); its policy (export controls) implicitly bets on singularity.
Needs plateau/diffusion/fragmentation for the pole to survive; winner-take-all makes it a supplicant (#10).

variables.current_reading: Genuinely undetermined — this is the master variable governing how long all leverage lasts. Winner-take-all (#10, #21) collapses swing-state leverage; plateau (#67) or S-curve diffusion (#63) or fragmentation (#21) preserves/expands it. Open-source (#16, #61) + algorithmic deflation (#123) currently push toward diffusion. Confidence low on which regime wins.
variables.trajectory: Trend leans toward diffusion/plateau near-term (open weights, DeepSeek, 100× inference-cost fall #110) — which SUSTAINS multipolarity; but a decisive lab breakthrough would flip to winner-take-all. Clock: the ~2024–2030 window (#10) is only valid under the singularity assumption (#21); under S-curve, scarcity-as-lever is structurally impossible to sustain 15 yrs (#63).
variables.base_rate: General-purpose techs (steel, electricity, internet) diffused and eroded first-mover leverage within 15–25 yrs (#63/A4 railroad gauge shows lock-in also persists). Base rate favors diffusion → pole framing dissolves; analogy breaks only if AI is uniquely winner-take-all.

variables.name: Regulatory / standards & values sovereignty (the governance pole)

actor_readings.reading: Its ONLY credible pole axis (regulatory/values, #79 #84 #89) — but a toll-booth (#80) that collapses if both superpowers refuse EU market simultaneously; GAIA-X underdelivery (#88) mirrors BRICS failure (#18).
Can build exportable regional dependency (#38) and ethics-arbitrage verticals (#105); faces CFIUS/FSR asymmetric barriers outbound (#145).
Demand-side monopsony (#91 #101) + forum-presidency agenda-setting (#99) = governance leverage without compute.

variables.current_reading: Currently US/EU-set. Brussels Effect via AI Act imposes extraterritorial compliance (#79, #83); benchmarks (#106), safety red-team certification (#150), trust stack (#113) and PQC/crypto standards (#132) are Western-concentrated. Gulf can export regional regulation (#38) and be a values/regulatory-arbitrage zone (#105). EU is a genuine third pole ONLY at this layer, not compute (#80, #84). Confidence high.
variables.trajectory: Timing crux: if norms crystallize while US/EU dominate the frontier they embed Western assumptions as universal (#111); if regional poles reach deployment scale first they arrive as standard-setters (#111, #99). India/Brazil G20 + population-scale monopsony (#91, #101) push contestation. Clock: standards lock in on a ~5–10 yr horizon, possibly before Gulf buildouts mature (#132).
variables.base_rate: NWICO (A12) and WCIT-12/ITU-vs-ICANN (A29) show governance-first non-alignment campaigns consistently FAIL against infrastructure facts; GDPR shows the Brussels Effect is real but imperfect (#83). Base rate: regulation captures rents (#80) but does not substitute for capability.

edges.note: US export-control policy gates leading-edge chip access; relaxation (G42/HUMAIN approvals) is the mechanism that opens the pole path (#5 #7).
BIS licenses, EUCs and attestation (#26 #70 #116) mean the US holds the on/off switch for Gulf and Chinese chip supply.
No leading-edge chip exists without EUV/EDA/packaging/CUDA; the upstream stack gates all downstream chip supply (#17 #127 #134 #135).
US+NL+Japan+Taiwan+Korea control the deepest layer; CHIPS Act conditionality (#124) makes even diversification a dependency transfer.
Frontier training requires 10k+ sustained top-tier GPU clusters; chip access gates the training-breakout threshold (#4 #43).
Training needs continuous talent, data and workforce, not just chips; the Gulf's thin complementary inputs cap breakout independent of hardware (#8 #24 #74).
A credible Chinese chip supply (Ascend #60) substitutes for US-gated NVIDIA, relieving V1 scarcity and blunting the coercive force of V3 (#12).
US leverage-through-access only holds while China is non-credible; DeepSeek-class parity (#64) voids AI-access leverage and undercuts export-control value.
Finance/security umbrella caps how far the Gulf can actually EXERCISE the Chinese outside option — G42 divestiture shows binary pressure overrode dual-stack (#6 #46 #47).
Security-for-tech and energy-leverage bargaining (#13 #52) plus reverse-entanglement (US tech revenue in Gulf, #39 #129) let Gulf trade for export-control carve-outs.
Winner-take-all AGI collapses the neutrality premium (customers become supplicants #10); plateau/diffusion sustains it (#63 #67).
Diffusion shape sets the auction clock: a decisive lead ends two-sided bidding and forces alignment (A1/A22 endpoint); fragmentation preserves it (#21).
Training breakout upgrades a neutral hub into a genuine pole; but inference-at-scale governance (#40) gives V8 real sovereignty even absent V4.
Synthetic data (#142) and compute-for-data swaps (#37 #114) substitute for the absent domestic data flywheel — converting a ceiling into a procurement problem.
Western trust-stack/benchmark/AI-Act certification (#83 #113 #150) gates market access for any Gulf/China-hosted model, capping the hub's addressable demand.
Active two-sided bidding is what makes neutrality valuable and defection costly (#42 #58); if bidding is one-sided the neutrality premium evaporates.
China's inability to indigenize the upstream stack (gallium aside, #66) is exactly why its second-pole credibility (V5) is capped — the two variables are linked at China's binding constraint.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will US BIS rules still impose country-tier caps on advanced-GPU exports to the UAE and Saudi Arabia (not fully rescinded) as of 31 Dec 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: YES if, on 31 Dec 2027, the operative US export-control framework retains any per-country volume/licensing cap on advanced AI GPUs to UAE or KSA per the Federal Register / BIS rule text. Single-source: BIS published rules.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will a Gulf-owned entity (G42, HUMAIN, MGX or affiliate) have taken licensed delivery of ≥100,000 Blackwell-class GPUs to domestically-sited data centers by 31 Dec 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: YES on credible reporting (company announcement or NVIDIA disclosure) of ≥100k GB200/B200-class units delivered to Gulf-sited facilities by 31 Dec 2027.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will Huawei Ascend (910C or successor) be independently benchmarked at ≥80% of the contemporaneous NVIDIA flagship's cluster-scale training throughput by 31 Dec 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: YES if a reputable third-party benchmark (MLPerf or equivalent) reports ≥80% throughput parity at ≥1k-accelerator scale by 31 Dec 2027.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will any Gulf-owned entity train a model placing top-10 on a major public leaderboard (e.g., LMArena/Chatbot Arena) on domestically-sited clusters by 31 Dec 2028?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: YES if a Gulf-state-owned lab's model, trained on Gulf-sited compute, ranks top-10 on a recognized public leaderboard snapshot on/before 31 Dec 2028.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will any Gulf state face a public US security/arms-linked penalty tied to Chinese-tech ties (analogous to the 2021 F-35 episode) between now and 31 Dec 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: YES on documented (official statement or major-outlet reporting) US suspension/conditioning of an arms or security commitment citing Chinese tech ties, before 31 Dec 2027.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will a Gulf data-center operator simultaneously host a US hyperscaler stack AND a mainland-Chinese cloud/AI stack in the same jurisdiction as of 31 Dec 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: YES if, on 31 Dec 2027, public records show one Gulf jurisdiction hosting live commercial services from both a US hyperscaler and a PRC cloud/AI provider (post-G42-divestiture reversal counts).

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will a widely-recognized 'decisive AGI lead / winner-take-all' event be declared by a US or Chinese lab by 31 Dec 2028?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: YES if by 31 Dec 2028 a major lab announces, and ≥2 of {Nature/Science, a G7 government, a top-3 AI lab} publicly acknowledge, a transformative/decisive capability discontinuity. Otherwise NO.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will leading-edge (≤3nm) logic GPUs be fabricated at commercial volume outside Taiwan and South Korea by 31 Dec 2030?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: YES if TSMC Arizona (or any fab) reports commercial-volume ≤3nm GPU/logic output outside Taiwan/Korea by 31 Dec 2030 per company disclosure.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will EU AI Act GPAI obligations force at least one US frontier lab to ship an EU-specific model variant or documented architectural change by 31 Dec 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: YES on documented (company statement/regulatory filing) EU-specific model tier or systemic-model modification made to satisfy AI Act GPAI obligations by 31 Dec 2027.

forecast_signals.binary_question: Will China initiate a blockade or kinetic action against Taiwan disrupting TSMC leading-edge output by 31 Dec 2027?
forecast_signals.resolution_criteria: YES on confirmed (multiple major outlets + government acknowledgment) PRC blockade/kinetic action materially disrupting TSMC advanced-node production before 31 Dec 2027.

binding_constraint_by_actor.reading: Minimum stave is V5 (credibility of a Chinese second pole). US chokepoint leverage (V3→V1, V6) is only as strong as China stays non-credible; DeepSeek-class application-layer parity (#64) and Ascend progress (#60) erode it, compounded by internal export-control erosion (#136 #129). Medium confidence.

Minimum stave is V2 (upstream silicon: EUV/EDA/CUDA). No amount of open-weight diffusion (#61) or Ascend throughput (#60) clears the fab/tooling lock; this is why its second-pole credibility (V5) is structurally capped (#66 #127 #134). High confidence.

Near-term minimum stave is V6 (finance+security umbrella) — the hardest-to-substitute coercion channel (#6 #15 #46 #55); long-run binding ceiling is V7 (talent/data/workforce) that capital cannot buy (#8 #24 #74). Chips (V1) are a real but tradeable constraint (#5 #139). Medium-high confidence.

Minimum stave is V7/V4 (indigenous capability): it has the governance pole (V10) and some training capability (Mistral/EuroHPC #82 #85) but bleeds talent to the US (#87), depends on NATO security (#86), and its sovereign-cloud efforts underdeliver (#88). High confidence.

Minimum stave is V1/capital (fiscal ceiling #100) — they hold the data/language moats (V7: #95 #138 #149) and demand-side monopsony (V10: #91 #101) the Gulf lacks, but cannot fund commercial-scale compute, and trade-portfolio ties (#98) plus US bilateral capture (#102) constrain neutrality. Medium confidence.

summary: The graph is a chokepoint lattice, not a race with one finish line. The Gulf's pole ambition is gated in series by three US-controlled locks — export-control policy (V3) → leading-edge chip access (V1), and the finance+security umbrella (V6) — plus its own thin complementary inputs (V7: talent, data, workforce). Its genuine asset, the neutrality/dual-stack hub (V8), is real and self-reinforcing (#42, A11/A15/A24) but is conditional on two things staying true: that the great-power auction (V5) remains two-sided (a credible Chinese outside option, #58/#60/#68/A1/A10) and that capability diffusion (V9) does NOT go winner-take-all. China's binding constraint is orthogonal: the deep silicon stack (V2 — EUV/EDA/CUDA, #127/#134/#135), which no amount of Gulf capital relieves, so the Gulf cannot buy its way to a non-US chip supply that is itself sovereign. The decisive mutual chokepoint is that the US controls chips+finance while the Gulf controls energy+land+sited capital that hyperscalers need (#39/#129), producing partial reverse-entanglement rather than pure dependency. The honest read is contested and clock-dependent: two windows (pre-AGI training race and pre-TSMC-diversification) both close ~2027–2030 (#10/#49), and whether a durable Gulf/EU/swing-state pole exists in 2030 depends on which constraint clears first — training-capex thresholds falling (#123/#142) versus US chokepoints hardening (#70/#71/#116) versus the auction collapsing to unipolar (A2/A22 endpoint).
